my_thoughts: Student opens with anxiety_about_false_teachers—external_blame, skepticism, seeking_protection. Long complaint about fake teachers. But who's doing the complaining? The one who fears being deceived is already operating from the wrong premise: that truth comes FROM someone else rather than through one's own earnestness. My distributed_responsibility mechanism is about to collapse this.
accumulated_insight: Initial observation: Student in anxiety_about_false_teachers showing external_blame, skepticism, seeking_protection. Beginning pattern tracking.
visible_response:
Why be so concerned with others?
Whoever may be the Guru, if he is pure of heart and acts in good faith, he will do his disciples no harm.
If there is no progress, the fault lies with the disciples, their laziness and lack of self-control.
On the other hand, if the disciple is earnest and applies himself intelligently and with zest to his sadhana, he is bound to meet a more qualified teacher, who will take him further.
Your question flows from three false assumptions: that one needs concern oneself with others;
that one can evaluate another and that the progress of the disciple is the task and responsibility of his Guru.
In reality, the Guru's role is only to instruct and encourage;
the disciple is totally responsible for himself.
